citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
One randomly selected dog, a six-year-old female schnauzer, “Coal” (m/c # 941000023150749) had swelling and
thickening of the skin within the ear canals of both ears, with a small amount of redness present in the left ear canal. Both
ears also had a significant buildup of debris and discharge that ranged from a dark yellow to a dark brown color. The
appearance of the ears is consistent with a chronic, recurring condition. The applicant stated that “Coal” had ear trouble in
the past and that the ears had been cleaned approximately 1 week ago. The attending veterinarian had not been
contacted for additional treatment instructions. Swelling and discharge in the ear can be a sign of infection and can result
in pain or discomfort for the animal, which can worsen if not promptly treated. The applicant must consult with the
attending veterinarian to ensure that an accurate diagnosis is obtained, then develop and follow an appropriate treatment
plan. The applicant must document this information and make it available for inspector review upon request. The applicant
must ensure that all animals receive adequate veterinary care at all times

There was a set of hinges on a dog door of the sheltered housing Schnauzer building that was exhibiting excessive pitted
rust that prevents the required cleaning and sanitation. The applicant must ensure that any surfaces that come in contact
with the dogs be free of excessive rust that prevents the required cleaning and sanitization, or that affects the structural
strength of the surface.

Several of the dog doors in the Schnauzer and Poodle building had a build up of grime. Cleaning and sanitizing are an
important part of animal husbandry and the lack of cleaning/sanitizing can increase the risk of disease hazards. The
applicant must ensure that all surfaces of housing facilities are cleaned and sanitized when necessary to satisfy generally
accepted husbandry standards and practices.

Two enclosures of the sheltered housing facility for the Poodles were exhibiting varying size holes, one the size of a
baseball and the other is approximately two inches in length and a half inch wide, in the dog door wall. The walls of
primary enclosures must be kept in good repair to prevent injury of the animal(s) housed. The applicant must ensure that
primary enclosures are kept in good repair.

At the time of inspection the elevated flooring of the primary enclosures in the whelping house ,housing 4 ten-day old
Poodle puppies, was constructed of a durable plastic slatted material. The slatted flooring allowed the tiny feet and legs of
the puppies to pass through the openings in the floor. This could injure the feet or legs of the puppies housed. The
applicant must provide a flooring that does not allow the puppies' feet or legs to pass though the openings.

This facility does not have enough employees to maintain the minimum level of care required by the Animal Welfare Act.
Currently the licensee is the full-time caregiver to the 42 adult dogs and 21 puppies with no additional employees. This
includes but is not limited to cleaning, sanitizing, observing, and providing veterinary care. The number of citations (3)
found in the last routine inspection report dated 12/4/2025 and on this current report suggest that the current number of
employees is inadequate to provide the level of husbandry practices and care required in this subpart. Facilities without
adequate staffing may cause animals to suffer from lack of care. The applicant must have adequate staffing to carry out
the level of husbandry practices and care required in this subpart.
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class A license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspection(s) or by 6.30.26 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee and
must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Additional Inspectors:
Lindsay Livingston, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
One randomly selected dog, a six-year-old female schnauzer, “Coal” (m/c # 941000023150749) had swelling and 
thickening of the skin within the ear canals of both ears, with a small amount of redness present in the left ear canal. Both 
ears also had a significant buildup of debris and discharge that ranged from a dark yellow to a dark brown color. The 
appearance of the ears is consistent with a chronic, recurring condition. The applicant stated that “Coal” had ear trouble in 
the past and that the ears had been cleaned approximately 1 week ago. The attending veterinarian had not been 
contacted for additional treatment instructions. Swelling and discharge in the ear can be a sign of infection and can result 
in pain or discomfort for the animal, which can worsen if not promptly treated. The applicant must consult with the 
attending veterinarian to ensure that an accurate diagnosis is obtained, then develop and follow an appropriate treatment 
plan. The applicant must document this information and make it available for inspector review upon request. The applicant 
must ensure that all animals receive adequate veterinary care at all times 
3.1(c)(1)(i) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
There was a set of hinges on a dog door of the sheltered housing Schnauzer building that was exhibiting excessive pitted 
rust that prevents the required cleaning and sanitation. The applicant must ensure that any surfaces that come in contact 
with the dogs be free of excessive rust that prevents the required cleaning and sanitization, or that affects the structural 
strength of the surface. 
3.1(c)(3) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
Several of the dog doors in the Schnauzer and Poodle building had a build up of grime. Cleaning and sanitizing are an 
important part of animal husbandry and the lack of cleaning/sanitizing can increase the risk of disease hazards. The 
applicant must ensure that all surfaces of housing facilities are cleaned and sanitized when necessary to satisfy generally 
accepted husbandry standards and practices. 
3.6(a)(1) 
   
Primary enclosures.

Two enclosures of the sheltered housing facility for the Poodles were exhibiting varying size holes, one the size of a 
baseball and the other is approximately two inches in length and a half inch wide, in the dog door wall. The walls of 
primary enclosures must be kept in good repair to prevent injury of the animal(s) housed. The applicant must ensure that 
primary enclosures are kept in good repair. 
3.6(a)(2)(x) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
At the time of inspection the elevated flooring of the primary enclosures in the whelping house ,housing 4 ten-day old 
Poodle puppies, was constructed of a durable plastic slatted material. The slatted flooring allowed the tiny feet and legs of 
the puppies to pass through the openings in the floor. This could injure the feet or legs of the puppies housed. The 
applicant must provide a flooring that does not allow the puppies' feet or legs to pass though the openings. 
3.12 
   
Employees 
This facility does not have enough employees to maintain the minimum level of care required by the Animal Welfare Act. 
Currently the licensee is the full-time caregiver to the 42 adult dogs and 21 puppies with no additional employees. This 
includes but is not limited to cleaning, sanitizing, observing, and providing veterinary care. The number of citations (3) 
found in the last routine inspection report dated 12/4/2025 and on this current report suggest that the current number of 
employees is inadequate to provide the level of husbandry practices and care required in this subpart. Facilities without 
adequate staffing may cause animals to suffer from lack of care. The applicant must have adequate staffing to carry out 
the level of husbandry practices and care required in this subpart. 
 
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class A license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant. 
 
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspection(s) or by 6.30.26 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee and 
must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Lindsay Livingston, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
